content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 14, 2017

Alan Seem
Shearman & Sterling LLP
1460 El Camino Real, 2nd Floor
Menlo Park, CA 94025

John Marchand, Mayor
City of Livermore
1052 S. Livermore Avenue
Livermore, California 94550

Jason Alcala, City Attorney
City of Livermore
1052 S. Livermore Avenue
Livermore, California 94550

Re:     Your Request for Advice
        Our File No. A-17-058

Dear Mr. Seem:

     This letter responds to your request for advice as counsel to the China Silicon Valley
Business Development ("CSV"), a California non-profit corporation, on behalf of John Marchand,
Mayor of the City of Livermore, regarding the gift provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").¹ Note that the Act imposes duties on public officials and on donors to the officials.²

     Please note that we are only providing advice under the gift and conflict of interest
provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common
law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Additionally, the Fair Political Practices Commission (the
"Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) I
FPPC Ops. 71), and this letter is based on the facts presented.

_______________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Section 89521 provides: "Any person who makes or receives an honorarium, gift, or expenditure in violation
of this chapter is liable in a civil action brought by the Commission for an amount of up to three times the amount of the
unlawful honorarium, gift, or expenditure.

File No. A-17-058
Page No. 2

QUESTION

Are payments by the Luzhou Municipal People's Government for the Mayor Marchand's trip to China subject to the gift limits set forth in the Act?

CONCLUSION

The travel, lodging and meal payments from the Luzhou Municipal People's Government are reportable gifts that are not subject to the gift limits pursuant to Section 89506(a)(2).

FACTS

CSV has invited Mayor Marchand to travel to China as part of the "March 2017 China Trip." You state that the purpose, sponsorship, and certain arrangements for this trip are similar to the Silicon Valley Mayors' China Trip June 2014 and the September 2015 also organized by CSV. We have previously issued letters regarding these trips and other China trips organized by CSV.3

CSV's purpose in organizing this trip is to facilitate investment and international trade, and promote communications between China and the Silicon Valley region. Mayor Marchand is scheduled to arrive in China on March 16, 2017 and visit Luzhou City, Sichuan Province. Mayor Marchand returns on March 22, 2017. While in China, he will have the opportunity to participate in meetings with local government officials, potential investors and CEOs from local companies. Specifically, he will be attending the 2017 China International Alcoholic Drinks Expo, hosted by Luzhou Municipal People's Government. The invitation states that the visit "can promote cooperation in wine industry, culture, economy and trade between the two cities" and will be a "global event for wine enterprises, distributors, and consumers."

You state with respect to financing, that the Luzhou Municipal People's Government, a local Chinese government authority, will pay for the following estimated expenses for Mayor Marchand:

• All international air travel expenses to and from China; $2,000 per person
• Remaining expenses for travel, lodging and meals within China; $3,000 per person

You state that "CSV will not provide any funds or services with monetary value to Mayor Marchand in connection with the March 2017 China Trip." You have made this request on behalf of the mayor, as the counsel to CSV. The mayor has indicated his participation is conditional on receiving a determination that travel can be accepted and reported as determined in previous informational letters.4

___________________________

3 Informational Assistance Letters, Seem No. I-16-236; No. I-16-079 and No. I-15-189, and collective requests for advice Reyes, et al Nos. A-14-071, A-14-072, A-14-078 and A-14-079.

4 Email from Jason R. Alcala, March 10, 2017.

File No. A-17-058
Page No. 3

ANALYSIS

The Act's Gift Limit Provisions:

A gift is defined under Section 82028(a) as:

. . . any payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received and includes a rebate or discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is made in the regular course of business to members of the public without regard to official status.

In an effort to reduce improper influences on public officials, the Act regulates the receipt of gifts by local public officials in three ways:

• Gift Limit of $470: The Act places limitations on the acceptance of gifts by certain public officials. The current limit is $470 from a single source in a calendar year. (Section 89503; Regulation 18940.2.) This gift limit applies to all public officials. (Sections 89503(a) and 87200.)

• Gift Reporting: So that the public is made aware of any potential influences from gifts, the Act imposes reporting obligations on certain public officials requiring that any gift (or any gifts that aggregate to $50 or more from the same source) received during the calendar year are disclosed on the officials' statements of economic interests. (Sections 87200 - 87210.)

• Conflict of Interest Disqualification: The Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using his or her position to influence the outcome of a governmental decision involving the donor of a gift or gifts with an aggregate value of $470 or more provided to, received by, or promised to the official within the 12 months prior to the date the decision is made. (Sections 87100 and 87103(e); Regulations 18700 and 18703.4.)

Under some circumstances, even though a payment for transportation, lodging, and subsistence may be a reportable gift under the Act, the gift is not subject to the Act's current $470 gift limit. In this regard, Section 89506(a) provides (in pertinent part) an exception from the gift limits for certain travel payments:

Payments, advances, or reimbursements, for travel, including actual transportation and related lodging and subsistence that is reasonably related to a legislative or governmental purpose, or to an issue of state, national, or international public policy, are not prohibited or limited by this chapter if either of the following apply:

(1) ....

(2) The travel is provided by a government, a governmental agency, a foreign government, a governmental authority, a bona fide public or private

File No. A-17-058
Page No. 4

educational institution, as defined in Section 203 of the Revenue and Taxation
Code, a nonprofit organization that is exempt from taxation under Section
501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code, or by a person domiciled outside the
United States who substantially satisfies the requirements for tax - exempt status
under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code.

Here, the travel is directly related to a legislative or governmental purpose and is provided
by a foreign government. The stated purpose of this trip is to facilitate investment and international
trade, promote communications between China and the Silicon Valley region. While in China,
Mayor Marchant will have opportunities to participate in meetings with local government officials,
potential investors, and CEOs from local companies. Luzhou Municipal People's Government will
pay for all international air travel expenses to and from China, as well as remaining expenses for
travel, lodging and meals within China during the trip. Consequently, the specific payments are
reportable gifts that are not subject to gift limits pursuant to Section 89506(a)(2). 5 We discuss the
conflict of interest issues further below.

Please note that any payments made for items other than travel and related lodging and
subsistence may be considered a reportable gift, subject to the $470 gift limit, and subject to the
conflict of interest provisions of the Act. For all gifts, if the official reimburses the donor or
donor's intermediary for the gift within 30 days, no gift is considered to have been received or
accepted. (Regulation 18941(c)(3)) Conflict of Interest Disqualification

As mentioned above, a public official who receives gift(s) of $470 or more may have a
financial conflict of interest under the Act. (Sections 87100 et seq.) The official must disqualify
himself or herself from voting or otherwise participating in a governmental decision affecting that
source, if the payment was received or promised to the official within 12 months preceding the
decision. Therefore, if the public official accepts gifts valued at $470 or more, they may be
prohibited from participating in governmental decisions affecting the source of those gifts.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: L. Karen Harrison
Counsel, Legal Division

5 Note that Regulation 18945(a) provides: "The person who makes the gift to the official(s) is the source of the
gift unless that person is acting as an intermediary. The person is acting as an intermediary for the source of the gift
when the gift to the official was provided under any of the following conditions: (1) the person receives a payment from
a source and the payment is made to the official after the source identifies the official as the intended recipient of the
gift; (2) the person receives a payment from a source after soliciting the payment with the understanding that the
payment will be used for the sole or primary purpose of making a gift to an official; or (3) the person receives a
payment from a source after the payment was solicited by the official or the official's agent for the purpose of making a
gift to the official."

sections.question:
Are payments by the Luzhou Municipal People's Government for the Mayor Marchand's 
trip to China subject to the gift limits set forth in the Act?
sections.conclusion:
The travel, lodging and meal payments from the Luzhou Municipal People's Government 
are reportable gifts that are not subject to the gift limits pursuant to Section 89506(a)(2).

CSV has invited Mayor Marchand to travel to China as part of the "March 2017 China

We have previously issued letters regarding these trips and other China trips organized by CSV.3 
CSV's purpose in organizing this trip is to facilitate investment and international trade, and 
to promote communications between China and the Silicon Valley region. Mayor Marchand is 
scheduled to arrive in China on March 16, 2017 and visit Luzhou City, Sichuan Province. Mayor 
Marchand returns on March 22, 2017. While in China, he will have the opportunity to participate in 
meetings with local government officials, potential investors and CEOs from local companies. 
Specifically, he will be attending the 2017 China International Alcoholic Drinks Expo, hosted by 
Luzhou Municipal People's Government. The invitation states that the visit "can promote 
cooperation in wine industry, culture, economy and trade between the two cities" and will be a

local Chinese government authority, will pay for the following estimated expenses for Mayor

the mayor, as the counsel to CSV. The mayor has indicated his participation is conditional on

informational letters.4 
3 Informational Assistance Letters, Seem No. 1-16-236; No. 1-16-079 and No. 1-15-189, and collective requests 
for advice Reyes, er al Nos. A-14-071, A-14-072, A-14-078 and A-14-079. 
4 Email from Jason R. Alcala, March 10, 2017.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Are payments by the Luzhou Municipal People's Government for the Mayor Marchand's 
trip to China subject to the gift limits set forth in the Act?

CONCLUSION: The travel, lodging and meal payments from the Luzhou Municipal People's Government 
are reportable gifts that are not subject to the gift limits pursuant to Section 89506(a)(2).
